Angry, dark cloud hovering low above skyscrapers.
LOWER MANHATTAN, NARROW SIDE STREET - NIGHT

A LOCAL TAVERN is closing for the night. The barmaid, CASSIE, is coming out, pulling a raincoat over her cocktail waitress uniform, the short ruffled skirt, the long dark stockings and flashy high heels.
Come on, Cassie. I now an after-hours joint. You've been on your feet long enough. Forget it.
I've got a day job, too.
She starts up the street waving cheerfully back at the regular customers.
Sure I can't give you a lift?
I'd rather fight off the muggers.
CAMERA TRUCKS with her as she continues up the street, in a quick, no-nonsense stride. Her heavy handbag stuffed with the
night's tips. VARIOUS SHOTS - EMPTY STREETS, LOWER MANHATTAN - NIGHT

One street narrower than the next. sound of those high-heeled shoes. We HEAR the click-clacking Then across one of these empty streets, a SHADOW darts, then another SHADOW following it, and still a THIRD. Some of the jungle creatures of the city are afoot.
TRUCKING SHOT - CLOSE ON CASSIE
She reaches into her pocket, takes out her house keys. Apparently she is approaching home.
HER POV TRUCKING ALONG THE STREET

Just ahead, her old apartment building, a converted brownstone, steps leading up to the entry way. CAMERA TRUCKING FORWARD telling us that this is Cassie's destination. She's almost home. She turns and looks to her left. HER POV TO THE LEFT We see a trash can rolling toward the curb and then a pack of ALLEY CATS dashing away form the site. The culprits. ANGLE ON CASSIE Relieved. CAMERA SWINGING BACK AND CIRCLING HER as she turns and comes face-to-face with TWO MEAN LOOKING HISPANIC TEENAGERS--dark and grinning viciously. Cassie knows what they want.
I worked all night for these tips, God damn you.

The boys curse at her in Spanish and lunge forward, reaching for the bag. Cassie swings the heavy handbag smacking one of them across the side of the head. He staggers for a moment and Cassie takes off in the opposite direction, SCREAMING her head off a she runs. There's no place to go but the narrow alley right behind her. LOW ANGLE - CASSIE RUNNING Looking up at the windows above. out. Help me! All shut. The lights are

Somebody help me! It's hard to run in those spiked heels. She stumbles falls against a wall, skins her arm, pushes off and continues running. Finally she stops, catches her breath, looks right and left. HER POV There's nobody there. There's nobody coming up the alley behind her and nobody up ahead. Maybe she's lost them. She takes a step forward, then another. Right behind her is a fence surrounding some site of excavation where a building is being demolished, and from over that fence drops one of the boys, leaping at her. She jumps out of the way. The boy misses her, sprawls at her feet. She heads back in the other direction. Her path is blocked by the other boy. She takes the only avenue of approach left, the last alleyway to her right. The muggers are right behind her. knife. One pulls a switchblade
ANGLE ON. WINDOW UP ABOVE THE ALLEY - NIGHT
A light comes on. A UGLY OLD MAN sticks his head out the window and shouts.
You muthafuckers. Leave her alone, you little bastards!
I see you.
EXT. ADJACENT STREET CORNER - NIGHT

Cassie running--realizing she's somehow lost her purse. looks back over her shoulder. She And she runs right into someone. The immediate SHOCK. We are so TIGHT ON HER that we can't see who it is that is holding her, but there is a powerful gloved hand gripping her shoulder.
Oh, oh my God, thank God it's you.
CAMERA BEGINS TO PULL BACK slowly. It's dark on this corner. We can't make out the features of the man but he's very tall, and wearing a uniform. It's a POLICEMAN with white gloves on.
They nearly caught me. (catches her breath) I dropped my bag back there with all my tips. (Cont.)
The policeman says nothing. All we hear is Cassie's heavy breathing. The cop simply holds her with an iron grip and she looks up at his shadowed face trying to make it out in the darkness. ,,---.
Aren't you going to do anything?

Shooting from the ground up, the cop holding Cassie seems immense as he looms over her. With his free hand, he reaches inside his uniform jacket. CAMERA MOVES IN on that hand as it comes out of the jacket clutching a long stiletto
We are accustomed to seeing cops with guns but not with knives. CLOSE-UP CASSIE as she too sees the knife.
What's that for? Please don't *** I didn't do anything *** wrong ***
CLOSE-UP THE COP'S HAND as the knife is raised to strike.
ANGLE ON THE CORNER - NIGHT

as the Hispanic boys who have pursued Cassie round the corner. They see Cassie's purse and pick it up. Their instinct is to run--but then they see the cop up ahead. They freeze in their tracks when they realize the cop has a knife in his hand. THEIR POV Distant. Cassie and the cop. The giant officer is hardly more than a silhouette. A ribbon of street light crosses Cassie allowing us to see the anguished expression on her face, the absolute fear and horror as she screams again for help. The huge cop is about to kill her. They've seen everything that can happen out on the streets. At least they thought they had.
But this evokes horror even in them.

ANGLE ON CASSIE AND THE COP She SCREAMS - The knife flashes through frame. ANGLE ON CASSIE'S LEGS As they go limp one of her high-heeled shoes topples off of her foot. Raised again and thrusting forward OUT OF FRAME. We hear the sound of the blade's brutal impact on the victim. ANGLE ON THE HISPANIC BOYS Their reaction.
Let's gol

They turn and run away in terror carrying Cassie's purse with them. The animals of the night have encountered a bigger, fiercer beast. ,..-.. The cop and his victim, Cassie, as he slowly allows her body to slide to the pavement into the pool of blood that has formed at her feet.
OMIT SCENE 24
24a CLOSE-UP CASSIE'S PURSE - CAMERA PULLS BACK TO REVEAL
EXT. - SAME STREET CORNER - THE SCENE OF THE CRIME - NIGHT
Now prowl cars surround the area. One of the Hispanic boys is being held by DETECTIVES. purse presented as evidence. The Boy is nearly hysterical as the cops slap him across the face with the purse.
Yeah, we got the purse! But we don't kill her! I swear to Jesus! We don't kill her! It was a cop! Shut the fuck up!
No. I no be quiet. It was the cop! This big cop with the knife!
AUTOPSY ROOM - DAY
SEVERAL OFFICERS AND DETECTIVES are looking at the remains of the victim. One of them is DET. LT. FRANK McCRAE, a craggy but handsome, hard-drinking, hard-working police veteran.
You can't tell me this much damage was done with a switchblade. DET. LOVEJOY has just entered.
He crosses to the examination table but tries not to look at the body. He's squeamish.
Bullshit. I just got a statement from an eyewitness who saw the kids chase her with knives. (a beat) Can't you cover her up?
Her name's Cassie Phillips. I used to stop in her place for a nightcap.
always had the latest jokes. She had a sense of humor. I always told her she ought to audition for one of those comedy places.
All the more reason I'd think you'd want her murderers put away. Will you pull up the sheet, please?

Don't pass out on me, Lovejoy. Look at the wounds. Cassie was five ten and a half and she had on high heels. The person who killed her slashed downward with a great deal of force. No, it'd have to be somebody well over six feet. Maybe six three just like both kids described. (Cont.) You don't really believe it was a cop.
Why not? She knew me. lot of cops. What's the use.
some fresh air. She knew a
Let's go. I need
Lovejoy quickly walks out, still trying to cover his nausea. The Asst. District Attorney turns to Mccrae.
I'm getting a ruling from the commissioner on this. I don't want you talking about this case to anybody.
The Asst. District Attorney leaves along with the others. Mccrae stands looking down at the corpse which is OFF CAMERA. He finally pulls the sheet up over the naked body.
You must've been so afraid, Cassie *** and then you saw a cop.
THE CITY - NIGHT
bars are closing, once again the night people of the city are drifting home. weaving its way through the downtown streets. music is blasting on the car stereo.
Hard rock CAR
SAM is at the wheel, his girlfriend NANCY is beside him. They're in their early twenties, and they're passing a bottle of beer back and forth between them.
I think we just about closed that club.
Sam, watch it, there's a light up ahead. (Cont.)

I see it. He puts on the brakes, the car pulls to a stop at the intersection. The traffic light as turned RED. as it draws to a stop and waits. ANGLE ON THE RED LIGHT It doesn't change. THE CAR What's the matter with that light. It's probably broken. Relax. What's the rush? She kisses him, nuzzles up. He finishes the beer. All at once there is a RAPPING on the roof of the car. CAMERA MOVES AROUND so we can see that someone is standing beside the car. A LARGE FIGURE IN A BLUE UNIFORM. And he's pounding on the car with his fist. Oh, shit, a cop. Ditch that beer! Where did he come from? Sam hides the beer bottle on the floor then rolls down the window. Tries to act sober.
officer. He wants you to get outl
The cop silently pulls the car door open. We notice now that his hands are covered with scar tissue.
Better cooperate, honey. asshole.
Oh, man, I can't stand any more tickets.

My insurance will go up. Sam gets out. vehicle, too. As he does, the beer bottle rolls out of the It doesn't matter WE SEE EVERYTHING NOW OVER NANCY'S SHOULDER FROM HER POV INSIDE THE CAR. THE ENTIRE SCENE IS PLAYED FROM THE SPECTATOR'S VIEWPOINT Sam bends down, picks up th~ beer bottle, the stern of which is now chipped. He hands the bottle sheepishly to the cop whose gloved hand is outstretched demanding the evidence. SAM
Just one lousy beer *** look--I'rn
sorry. We see the immense cop march Sam directly in front of the vehicle so he's almost spotlighted by the headlights of the car.

The car stereo continues to blast away with hard rock music to further obliterate any possibility of hearing what's being said out there. We see Sam begin to go through the motions of the sobriety test, reaching out and touching his fingertips together. Now, closing his eyes and beginning to walk a straight line. The huge cop hovers close by watching him, dominating him, and always out of the lights of the car so he remains little more than a silhouette. (We always have him BACKLIT.) CLOSE- UP NANCY watching, becoming restless. ANGLE OVER NANCY'S SHOULDER AGAIN WE SEE EVERYTHING THROUGH THE WINDSHIELD Sam walking that straight line, holding his hands out with his eyes closed. Then the big cop steps in front of Sarn--blocking him from Nancy's view. He makes several quick violent motions. Then whirls--tossing Sam's body through the air onto the hood of the car--straight at Nnacy. The windshield cracks and is instantly covered with blood. CLOSE-UP - NANCY Her stunned reaction. Blood continuing to spray onto the glass obliterating our view. We can only barely see Sam's body still flailing out there on the hood--but we still don't know what was done to him. CLOSE-UP - NANCY SCREAMING hideously now, HONKING the horn furiously. The figure of the giant cop vanishing down the street. It finally turns green. GO.
ANGLE ON NANCY INSIDE THE VEHICLE

Not knowing what to do, she slides behind the driver's seat. The windshield is covered with blood. She switches on the windshield wipers. SQUEAK, SQUEAK, SQUISH, SQUISH as the windshield wipers slash at the blood-covered windshield trying to create some vision. Nancy starts the engine and the car begins to roll.
THE CAR IN MOTION - NIGHT

The bleeding corpse of Sam slides off of the hood onto the ground rolling into The eyes wide in surprise. The mouth a huge gaping opening in the head with the bottom portion of the beer bottle protruding.
INT. * COMMISSIONER PIKE'S OFFICE
Frank Mccrae with COMMISSIONER PIKE, a big red-faced ex-cop who knows how to play the game of politics. A distant sign on the Commissioner's wall proclaims: "DRINK AND DRIVE. THAT Is MURDER."
You're immediately assuming this was a police officer--rather than just someone dressed up like a cop. McCRAE
He didn't go after the wife. He let her live. He wanted her to be a witness.
There you go! He's trying to discredit the police force. You see, that fits right into my profile that it's not a cop at all.

All the same, we better start checking on our own people. I'd like the police psychologist to give a rundown on any officers who have exhibited mental disturbance, anybody that's contemplated suicide or been under extreme emotional stress.
Why not give the entire force a sanity test while you're at it?
We're looking for a white man well over six feet tall without an alibi.
All right, but I want this kept within the department. Keep it quiet.
For how long? He'll kill again. enjoys killing. You seem to know alot about this man, don't you? Maybe we better check on your mental health one of these days. McCRAE
Any time, sir.
Weren't you the one that shot himself *two years back?
The gun went off.
CLOSE-UP McCRAE
He gives Pike a broad smile that has no joy or humor--but is more like a wild animal baring its teeth.
52ND STREET, MANHATTAN - NIGHT

A line up of jazz clubs but it's 4 a.m. and they're all closed now. The MUSICIANS are just leaving, carrying their musical instruments with them. One of them is EDDIE BAKER, a black guy, a bass player, and he is accustomed to carrying the huge bass zipped up in its cloth case. He hauls it down the street to his parked car, unlocks the trunk, and is just putting it into the trunk when he sees a cop stepping out of the shadows.
I'm not ripping nothing off. is my car.
The cop is moving closer. in the dark. His features still undiscernible
Shit. You wanna see my cabaret license? Wanna see my union card? (a beat) Well, you want something.
The cop reaches out his gloved hand. He's extending a pair of handcuffs--open, ready to be snapped on.
Ah, com'on, buddy. Don't arrest me.
CLOSE-UP THE HANDCUFFS as the cop shakes them more insistently. With his other hand he makes a circle signifying that Eddie should turn around. Turning around, complying.
Okay, take me in. You're going to feel fuckin' stupid!
as the cop handcuffs him quickly, both hands behind his back. Then turns him around, roughly.
Jesus, what happened to your face? No wonder you're such a mean son of a bitch.
The cop raises his billy club so that it comes into FRAME. At first Eddie flinches as if he thinks the club is going to strike him.
CLOSE-UP THE CLUB

as the gloved hands of the huge cop begin to unscrew the billy c-lub. The billy club comes apart. The larger portion of it really a wooden sheath and when it is removed reveals a long stiletto knife which we have seen in operation before. CLOSE-UP EDDIE'S FACE His reaction as he steps back.
Oh, no.

Then the cop's HAND ENTERS FRAME, shoves Eddie, pushes him away. Eddie stumbles back a few steps, looks to his right and his left as if hoping someone will come along. The street is completely empty. All the clubs are closed up. The lights are out. It's not a residential neighborhood. It's all stores and businesses and night spots. The cop shoves Eddie again, pushing him a few feet further away as if giving him a chance to run.
You ain't letting me go. You're playing with me. (then he yells) Help! For God's sake, help me,
somebody. Help!
LONG SHOT - THE STREET
as Eddie turns, looking up at the buildings, most of which have no windows, they are simply the backs of other old buildings, billboards and sheer brick walls.
ANGLE ON EDDIE

as he looks back toward the cops. CAMERA PANS to where the cop was standing, but he's gone. He's vanished as quickly as he came. Eddie turns an entire circle looking for him but he's not there. And then Eddie takes off in a dead run right up the center of the street.
There's a cop down here with a knifel. Somebody's gotta help me!
ANGLE ON DOORWAY OF BUILDING

Eddie runs up, throws his body against the doorway. There's a doorknob there but Eddie can't turn it. His hands are cuffed behind him. He can't ring the doorbell. He runs to the next doorway. It's the doorway to a restaurant and it's closed. Then he stops and listens and he hears FOOTSTEPS approaching.
Get away from me!
WIDER SHOT

as Eddie tears off across the street running to the store just diagonal. He uses his foot to kick on the door. Glass shatters. A BURGLAR ALARM GOES OFF. Now, maybe somebody will come. Eddie runs up the adjoining steps to a brownstone type building. Bounces his head, his body, his shoulders, every way against the door. He begins to press his shoulder against the buzzers and this time he can reach them and they ring *.* buzz, buzz, buzz.
Hurry up--hurry up! Answer! (Cont.)
A VOICE comes through.
Help mel That psycho that's killing people is a cop! Listen to me. It's a copl Buzz me in. Please buzz me in. Fuck off.
WIDE ANGLE - THE STREET

Eddie runs back down the steps in panic--loses his balance. A construction crew has torn up part of the street and laid fresh cement. Eddie trips and tumbles off the curb into this wet cement. lying face deep in the cement, trying to get up. The Maniac Cop moves quickly into FRAME, pushing Eddie's face down in the cement--drowning him in cement. Holding him there until he stops struggling. CAMERA PANS to the "Caution--Men At Work" sign.
EXT. THAT STREET - DAWN

Eddie's body now encased in hardened cement--remains face down. He's like a solid part of the street. Now police and workmen drill around him trying to dislodge his corpse. Chopping with pickaxes, trying to chip the corpse free of his all too visible grave.
JAKE'S SALOON
An Irish bar which for 80 years has been a hangout for policemen and newspapermen.
Mccrae is waiting, hoisting a few stiff qrinks.
by TOM SHEPARD, a veteran newsman.
Hello, Frank. Sorry I'm late. to tape a special feature for tonight's eleven o'clock news. Those sonsabitches are back on those same street corners in Harlem selling crack.
I've got a better story for you.
You know in fifteen years I don't think you've ever leaked anything. You're the most tight-mouthed bastard. What happened?
Three homiciaes this week by the same psychopath.
Another Son of Sam?
Worse.
This one's a cop.
You'r~ not shitting me?
A cop with a knife. There are plenty of witnesses. The department wants to stonewall it.
(laughs) I can't imagine why.
People have to be warned. The average citizen respects the uniform. They' 11
do whatever a cop tells them. Even walk up a dark alley.
Who is he killing? Pushers? Hustlers?
He only kills innocent people.
Jesus! A. "maniac cop." for a tag? How's that (Cont.)
Sure. Sensationalize it. Make it bigger than AIDS. That's what has to happen before city hall moves its ass.
You're ready to go on the line on this? Be quoted directly?
Mccrae doesn't answer. He swallows his whiskey in one gulp.
This may not be the place to talk. Come on, I'll buy a quart of Jack Daniels and we'll go back to my office.
Both men get up.
I have to have backup on every detail. The names of the witnesses. Specifics from the Medical Examiner's office. McCRAE I've got it all in my pocket, and I'll buy my own whiskey. Thanks.
Mccrae throws down some money on the table. The two men exit from the bar passing hordes of cops who are drinking, laughing it up.
It's going to.be a lot quieter in here after tonight.
They exit, leaving the off-duty cops still drinking, swearing, telling jokes and watching the ball game on TV. They will be a much soberer lot tomorrow.
CLOSE-UP TV SCREEN
Newsman Tom Shepard doing his late night broadcast.

Now a News Channel exclusive. Earlier tonight this reporter was informed by a source within the New York Police Department of attempts by the Homicide Division to suppress facts regarding three recent homicides. Facts and witnesses to these murders point unmistakably to (more) (cont.)

a uniformed police officer. I repeat, a uniformed New York cop has been seen in the commission of these brutal killings that occur on the streets of the city after dark. The question this network wants answered, why has Commissioner Pike withheld these facts, and does the mayor know what is going on in his city?
He's watching this on television. Shepard's voice

How can the public defend themselves if they're unaware that one of New York's finest may indeed be a psychopath whose intent is to kill them rather than protect them? Tomorrow on the morning show, we will have interviews with some of the witnesses as well as tape coverage with Detective Lieutenant Frank Mccrae who is in charge of the investigation and who was ordered not to speak to the press.
Goddamnitl Why can't that sonofabitch Pike control his own men?
Do you want McCrae's ass in here?

Shit nol The last thing I need is to be accused of a coverup. Issue a statement that I didn't know about the connection between these killings. And that I'm setting up an inquiry board headed by a team of independent psychologists.
How about saying something nice about the police? I mean the average cop on the beat. They're the one's that are going to take the heat on this. MAYOR
That's their lookout.
Let him handle the investigation. If he fails, I'll bust him. If he succeeds, I'll commend him, then six or eight months later, I'll cut his balls off.
CLOSE SHOT - A WIRE CLOTHES HANGER AT CAR WINDOW

pushing through the crack, trying to use the hook part to pull up the button--to gain admittance to the car. Performing the action is a WOMAN in her middle thirties, well dressed. She's just about got it when a VOICE calls out.
Hold it right there.
ANGLE ON THE CAR AND THE WOMAN she freezes. looks over her shoulder and sees a cop approaching. It's an ORDINARY OFFICER, clearly not our "Maniac." WOMAN

And then she turns abruptly. She's got a gun. Without warning she opens fire. She hits the cop at point-blank range before the cop can respond. CLOSE-UP THE WOMAN blasting away. Her eyes wide with fear.
You're not gonna do me!
COMMISSIONER PIKE'S OFFICE - DAY
Commissioner Pike, POLICE CAPTAIN RIPLEY, an exhausted black man in his fifties, and IRATE MEMBERS OF THE CITY COUNCIL.
Anybody who wants to shoot a cop these days has got a helluva excuse. She "thought" he was the maniac.
Look. We've received seven hundred letters accusing individual police officers.
A cop gives you a parking ticket so naturally you write in a letter accusing him of being the killer! Shit! Why does that have to happen four weeks short of my retirement?
We're all in trouble, Captain. Tourism in the city has dropped by
forty three percent, theater and
If you can't handle this, Ripley *** put in for early retirement and step down.
Not a fucking chance. I intend to get the bastard. But he's always a step ahead of me. If we stake out
Tribecca and Soho, he strikes on
He may be getting information from inside the department.
Oh, God, that means he really is-one of us.
"MANIC COP CLAIMS FOURTH VICTIM--ONITED NATIONS DELEGATE SLAIN." A scissors cut across the newspaper--clipping the story out.
BROOKLYN APARTMENT - NIGHT The residence of Jack Forrest and his wife Ellen.
ELLEN, a small pretty woman of 28, is seated alone at the kitchen table clipping out the story and hiding it under a stack of magazines. CAMERA PANS to the bedroom of the flat.
CLOSE-UP - A POLICE UNIFORM

hanging on the inside door of a closet. A HAND comes in and removes the uniform. CAMERA PULLS back as JACK FORREST at center of frame begins to get dressed. He's a big, handsome guy, well over six feet tall and he's wearing the regular blue, traditional uniform of the NYPD. While he's dressing, Ellen crosses into the room and watches him.
I didn't know you were on*auty tonight.
With all the flu going around, they had to put a lot of us on overtime.
Always at night. It worries me when you work so much at night.
It worries me too.
You never talk about it much.
When I come back, I like to forget.
But it makes me feel left-out. used to talk to me, Jack.
You're the one who dropped out of the therapy. I was willing to go. I was willing to pay the bills.
So it's all my fault now.
I tried to do everything to hold this marriage together. (Cont.)
You talk like it's over.
It's not enough to talk, you have
to listen too.
I suppose that's why you've been taking all this overtime. To get away from me. Or is it something else?
What do you mean?
Sometimes in the night, you wake up screaming, like you can't breath. Sometimes it scares me like you might hurt me in your sleep.
Have I ever hurt you?
I know it's wrong to be afraid of my own husband but ***
Why wouldn't you say that to the therapist? Why wouldn't you simply say, I'm afraid of him and I don't know why?
Don't, Jack. Don't go out tonight. Stay with me.
How can I? I'm on the duty roster. I'll make it up to you on the weekend. We'll take a ride in the country. We'll go up to Tarrytown like we used to.
But when Jack puts his arms around her he notices that she shudders.
Don't pull away from me. (Cont.)
I don't know what's happening to me. I just don't like being alone here in the apartment when you're out till all hours. I hear things.
I hear things.
There are enough locks on the doors, and you've got a gun and you know how to u-se it.
Here, let me button that for you.
If I come back early, I'll try not to wake you.
No, please wake me. Maybe I'll feel better. Maybe I'm getting too much sleep. They say that can be depressing to a person. Too much sleep.
Jack is now fully dressed in his policeman's uniform. He gets his billy club, his service revolver and puts it in the holster, and he is ready to go. She walks him to the door. ELLEN
You say that every night.
He unlocks the multiple locks on the door and goes out. She closes the door after him, locking each lock meticulously. The phone rings insistently. She answers it.
Hello?
He went out again, didn't he? Who is this? me?
Why do you keep calling
Why does Jack keep killing people? He going to do it again tonight? (Cont.)
Then the phone clicks dead.
Why are you doing this to me?

Then suddenly she hangs up--runs to the window--she can still see Jack crossing the street. Ellen runs to the closet and gets her own coat. She reaches up to the top shelf, opens a shoe box, takes down a small pistol. She puts it in her pocket. She unlocks the door and goes out, fast.
EXT. STREET
Ellen running out of the apartment building, following the same path that her husband just took.
EXT. ANOTHER STREET

Jack walking casually past intermittent traffic. CAMERA PANS back across the street to Ellen. She catches sight of him. Keeps her distance. CAMERA PANS to the newsstand beside her. More banner headlines: "MANIAC COP EXPECTED TO KILL AGAIN." In another: "POLICE PURGE RANKS IN SEARCH FOR MANIAC."
BACK TO WIDER SHOT OF STREET

Ellen continues in her pursuit of her husband. We can see him vanishing around a corner. Down the street laden with bars and after hours joints. Ellen stays far enough back so as not to be seen. A couple of grifters working a 3 Card Monte game fold up their cardboard boxes and flee when they see the cop coming. But this particular uniformed officer isn't on duty. It's obvious from the way he's walking that he's headed someplace in particular. Ellen stays right behind him. CAMERA FOLLOWS. There is a three story building with a flashing neon sign:
MOTEL--FREE PARKING.

It is this building that Jack is approaching. One of the letters in the electric sign is defective. flickers on and off and buzzes intermittently. It The color of the pink neon casts a strange pall over the parking lot and the doorways.
MOTEL - TIGHTER SHOT - NIGHT

Jack crosses the parking lot, bypassing the office of the motel and heads directly toward room 3 on the first floor. CLOSE SHOT ON ELLEN watching from the other end of the parking lot. HER POV as Jack pauses at the door to room 3, listens, waits a moment, then very quietly reaches for the doorknob, opens the door which is unlocked. He steps into the darkened room. He does not turn on the lights. He closes the door. CLOSE ON ELLEN It's all quite terrifying. Who is inside that motel room? CAMERA MOVES with her as she too crosses the parking lot moving between the tightly parked vehicles and drawing closer and closer to room 3. And what is Jack doing here? LOW ANGLE ON THE LINE UP OF DOORS Ellen approaches room 3. She listens outside. She touches the handle of the door. It is now locked. She moves to the window but the shade has been pulled. TIGHT ON ELLEN outside that window, attempting to peer through that tiny crack between the shade and the window. Then a HAND reaches into FRAME and grabs her by the shoulder. She almost SCREAMS out. WIDER ANGLE Revealing the MOTEL MANAGER. An elderly black man.
Need help, little lady?
I *** I locked myself out.
key in the room. Left the
No problem. Happens all the time. Still checking out in the morning?
I think so. (Cont.) Be out by eleven, else we charge for
another half day.
